message:
fix(scorecard-adapter): prevent status:undefined in output and fix test ID

- Fix testToArtifactItem to check mapped status value, not input, to avoid
  including status:undefined for in_progress tests
- Fix assumption ID mismatch in test (A-RS20251230 → A-RS-20251230)
- Add missing saveSessionCritiques(session1, []) for consistency

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"brenner_bot-70b","title":"Epic: TanStack Library Integration","description":"# Epic: TanStack Library Integration

## Vision
Transform the Brenner Bot web app's data layer, rendering performance, and state management by adopting the TanStack ecosystem. This is a strategic investment that will:
- **Eliminate redundant work**: Stop re-parsing the 473KB transcript on every page load
- **Improve performance**: Virtualize long document rendering (236 transcript sections to ~10 visible)
- **Enhance UX**: Persist reading positions, filter states, and user preferences
- **Reduce code complexity**: Replace ~500 lines of manual caching/state logic with battle-tested libraries

## Why TanStack?
TanStack libraries are the gold standard for React data management:
1. **Designed to work together**: Query, Virtual, Table, Form, Store share patterns
2. **Framework-agnostic core**: Not locked to React, future-proof
3. **Excellent TypeScript support**: Better DX than alternatives
4. **Active maintenance**: Widely adopted (40M+ downloads/week)
5. **Small bundle impact**: ~40KB gzipped total for all libraries we need

## Strategic Context
The Brenner Bot web app is reading-first. Users spend most time:
1. Reading the 236-section transcript
2. Comparing 3 model distillations
3. Browsing the quote bank
4. (Less frequently) Creating research sessions

## Success Metrics
1. Transcript page TTI < 1s (currently ~3s on slow networks)
2. Bundle size: < 50KB gzipped additional
3. Code reduction: Remove ~500 lines of manual cache/state logic
4. UX: Reading position persists across sessions","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-29T23:14:24.042946054-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-30T00:16:35.215601979-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-30T00:16:35.215601979-05:00","close_reason":"TanStack foundation complete: QueryClient + HydrationBoundary, corpus hooks (useCorpusDoc, useCorpusList, useCorpusSearch), search engine with MiniSearch, virtualized TranscriptViewer. Remaining P2 tasks (Store, Form, more Virtual) are incremental."}
 {"id":"brenner_bot-719","title":"Task: Build search index generation script","description":"Create script to generate search index at build time.

## Purpose

Build-time index generation ensures:
- Fast search (no runtime parsing)
- Consistent index across environments
- Index can be cached/CDN'd

## Requirements

### Input
- All corpus documents (7 files)
- Parse each by type (transcript, distillation, quote-bank, metaprompt)

### Processing
- Extract searchable content
- Split into appropriate chunks (sections, paragraphs)
- Preserve metadata: docId, title, section, anchor

### Output
- Search index file(s) in format required by chosen library
- Place in public/ or generated/ directory
- JSON format for easy inspection/debugging

## Implementation

### Script Location
`apps/web/scripts/build-search-index.ts`

### Integration Points
- Add to build process: `prebuild` script in package.json
- Ensure index regenerates when corpus changes
- Consider watching corpus files in dev mode

### Document Processing

Each document type needs specific handling:
- **Transcript**: 236 numbered sections
- **Distillations**: Sections with headers
- **Quote Bank**: Individual quotes
- **Metaprompts**: Full document or sections

### Index Entry Schema
```typescript
interface SearchEntry {
  id: string;           // unique identifier
  docId: string;        // document ID for linking
  docTitle: string;     // document title for display
  sectionTitle?: string; // section header if applicable
  content: string;      // searchable text
  anchor?: string;      // URL anchor for deep linking
  type: 'transcript' | 'distillation' | 'quote-bank' | 'metaprompt';
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Script generates valid index
- [ ] All 7 documents indexed
- [ ] Index includes section-level granularity
- [ ] Anchors enable deep linking
- [ ] Script runs as part of build
- [ ] Index size documented","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-29T22:29:20.035530266-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-30T00:06:14.416930481-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-30T00:06:14.416930481-05:00","close_reason":"scripts/build-search-index.ts is complete - generates MiniSearch index at build time for all 7 corpus documents with section-level granularity and anchors","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-719","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-gnj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-29T22:30:22.401126205-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-719","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-5i2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-29T22:30:22.463868729-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"brenner_bot-751","title":"CLI: ignore invalid implicit config file","description":"## Problem
`brenner.ts` loads a default config file path (`~/.config/brenner/config.json` or `%APPDATA%\brenner\config.json`) even when the user did not explicitly opt in. If that file exists but is malformed JSON (or has wrong shape), the CLI hard-fails.

## Why this is bad
From a first-principles UX/reliability standpoint:
- The *default* behavior should be resilient: a stray/half-written config file should not brick the CLI.
- Explicit config (`--config` or `BRENNER_CONFIG_PATH`) should remain strict (fail fast) to avoid silently ignoring operator intent.

## Root cause
`loadBrennerConfig()` parses config unconditionally when the default path exists; parse errors throw.

## Fix
- If config path is **implicit** (default) and parsing fails, **fail-soft**: ignore the config and continue with env + defaults.
- If config path is **explicit** (`--config` or `BRENNER_CONFIG_PATH`), keep **fail-fast** behavior.
- Optionally emit a one-line warning to stderr when ignoring an implicit config (must not spam normal output).

## Acceptance
- `--config <path>` with invalid JSON => exit 1 with a clear parse error.
- Default path exists but invalid JSON => CLI still works (exit codes/outputs unchanged for normal commands).
- Unit tests cover the explicit/implicit behavior boundary.
","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-30T06:30:32.06869927-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-30T06:51:12.122426712-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-30T06:51:12.122426712-05:00","close_reason":"Fail-soft on invalid implicit config; fail-fast on explicit config; add bun tests","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-751","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-5so.5.1.2","type":"discovered-from","created_at":"2025-12-30T06:30:32.073465143-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
-{"id":"brenner_bot-77nk","title":"Task: Add interactive mode to "brenner test execute"","description":"# Goal
Add an interactive prompt flow for `brenner test execute TEST_ID` so operators can record executions without manually specifying every flag.

This is a UX layer on top of the existing non-interactive (flag-driven) implementation.

---

# Background / Why
`brenner test execute` is the bridge between an executed discriminative test and hypothesis state updates (confirm/refute). In practice, the operator often has the observation in hand but doesn’t want to translate it into multiple CLI flags.

We already have:
- test records + execution recording (stored under `.research/tests/`)
- transition suggestions (kill/validate)
- optional auto-apply (`--apply`)

Missing: a first-class, low-friction operator UX for entering those fields interactively.

---

# Proposed UX (interactive mode)
When invoked as:

```bash
brenner test execute T-...
```

Prompt for:
1) Observed result/observation (free text)
2) Potency check: pass/fail (required)
3) Confidence: high/medium/low/speculative (default: medium)
4) Optional notes
5) (Optional / v0) “Apply suggested transitions?” (yes/no)
   - If yes: apply all or review one-by-one

Non-goal: running experiments. This command only records and binds results.

---

# CLI surface decisions
- Keep the existing flag-driven path working exactly as-is.
- Add `--interactive` to force prompts even if some flags are present.
- Add `--no-interactive` (or equivalent) to fail fast if required flags are missing (useful for scripts/CI).
- Ensure `--json` works in both modes (final output emitted as one JSON object).

---

# Implementation constraints
- Bun-only; no vendor AI APIs.
- Avoid adding heavy prompt dependencies if possible; prefer `node:readline`/stdin-based prompting.
- Ensure prompts do not trigger in non-TTY contexts unless explicitly requested (avoid hanging pipelines).

---

# Testing
Add CLI tests that drive stdin for interactive prompts. Approach:
- Extend the `runCli(...)` helper in `brenner.test.ts` to optionally write to stdin.
- Create a minimal temp project with a ready test + active hypotheses.
- Run `brenner test execute T-... --json` with scripted stdin input and assert:
  - execution recorded
  - suggestions returned
  - (if apply chosen) hypothesis states updated

---

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `brenner test execute TEST_ID` prompts for required execution fields when run interactively.
- [ ] Non-interactive usage via flags continues to work unchanged.
- [ ] Non-TTY runs do not hang (either require flags or require `--interactive`).
- [ ] Tests cover at least one interactive happy path (stdin-driven).","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-31T03:54:37.42559785-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-31T03:55:30.467538418-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-77nk","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-gson","type":"discovered-from","created_at":"2025-12-31T03:54:37.450934929-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"brenner_bot-77t2","title":"Task: Test Data Fixtures Library","description":"# Goal
Create a comprehensive test data fixtures library for consistent, realistic test data.

# Fixture Categories

## 1. Document Fixtures
Location: `apps/web/src/__fixtures__/documents/`

```typescript
// distillation.fixture.ts
export const distillationFixture = {
  id: 'test-distillation',
  type: 'distillation',
  title: 'Test Distillation Document',
  sections: [
    {
      number: 1,
      text: 'This is the first section with some Brenner concepts.',
      anchors: ['§1'],
    },
    {
      number: 103,
      text: 'You\'ve forgotten there\'s a third alternative. Both could be wrong.',
      anchors: ['§103'],
      jargonTerms: ['third alternative'],
    },
    // ... more sections
  ],
};

// transcript.fixture.ts
export const transcriptFixture = {
  id: 'test-transcript',
  type: 'transcript',
  title: 'Test Transcript',
  paragraphs: [
    { number: 1, text: 'First paragraph of transcript.' },
    // ... more paragraphs
  ],
};

// quotebank.fixture.ts
export const quoteBankFixture = {
  quotes: [
    {
      id: 'q1',
      text: 'It is good to be ignorant about a new field.',
      section: '§230',
      tags: ['productive-ignorance'],
    },
    // ... more quotes
  ],
};
```

## 2. Session Fixtures
Location: `apps/web/src/__fixtures__/sessions/`

```typescript
// session.fixture.ts
export const activeSessionFixture = {
  id: 'test-session-active',
  status: 'active',
  research_question: 'Does role-separated prompting improve artifact quality?',
  excerpts: ['§103', '§105', '§230'],
  created_at: '2025-12-30T12:00:00Z',
  participants: [
    { agent: 'BlackCastle', model: 'opus-4.5' },
    { agent: 'PurpleHill', model: 'opus-4.5' },
  ],
  contributions: [],
};

export const completedSessionFixture = {
  id: 'test-session-complete',
  status: 'completed',
  // ... includes full artifact
};

export const errorSessionFixture = {
  id: 'test-session-error',
  status: 'error',
  error_message: 'Agent Mail connection failed',
};
```

## 3. User Fixtures
Location: `apps/web/src/__fixtures__/users/`

```typescript
export const authenticatedUserFixture = {
  id: 'test-user',
  email: 'test@example.com',
  name: 'Test User',
  role: 'researcher',
};

export const adminUserFixture = {
  id: 'admin-user',
  email: 'admin@example.com',
  name: 'Admin User',
  role: 'admin',
};
```

## 4. API Response Fixtures
Location: `apps/web/src/__fixtures__/api/`

```typescript
// agent-mail.fixture.ts
export const agentMailInboxFixture = {
  result: [
    {
      id: 1,
      subject: 'Test message',
      from: 'TestAgent',
      to: ['CurrentAgent'],
      body_md: '## Test\n\nThis is a test message.',
      importance: 'normal',
      created_ts: '2025-12-30T12:00:00Z',
    },
  ],
};

export const agentMailErrorFixture = {
  error: {
    code: -32000,
    message: 'Agent not found',
  },
};
```

## 5. Error Response Fixtures
```typescript
export const error404Fixture = {
  status: 404,
  error: 'Not Found',
  message: 'The requested resource was not found.',
};

export const error500Fixture = {
  status: 500,
  error: 'Internal Server Error',
  message: 'An unexpected error occurred.',
};
```

# Fixture Factory Functions
```typescript
// factories.ts
export function createSessionFixture(overrides: Partial<Session> = {}): Session {
  return {
    id: `session-${Date.now()}`,
    status: 'pending',
    research_question: 'Default research question',
    excerpts: [],
    created_at: new Date().toISOString(),
    ...overrides,
  };
}

export function createDocumentFixture(type: DocType, overrides = {}): Document {
  // ... factory implementation
}
```

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Document fixtures for all document types
- [ ] Session fixtures for all states
- [ ] User fixtures with different roles
- [ ] API response fixtures (success and error)
- [ ] Factory functions for dynamic fixture creation
- [ ] TypeScript types for all fixtures
- [ ] Documentation for fixture usage","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-30T17:54:32.06709461-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-30T23:04:59.953396341-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-30T23:04:59.953396341-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented comprehensive test fixtures library with documents, sessions, users, API responses, and factory functions. 55 tests all passing.","labels":["testing infrastructure fixtures"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"brenner_bot-77t2","depends_on_id":"brenner_bot-4ep3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-30T22:37:11.886820429-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"brenner_bot-7b6","title":"Add /corpus index page (doc listing + filters)","description":"# Task

Create `/corpus` index page that lists corpus documents with search + filtering **and** respects the content policy.

Key constraint:
- In **public mode**, only show allowlisted docs (see `brenner_bot-5so.7.6.3`).
- For restricted docs (e.g., full transcripts), either hide them or show a locked stub that requires lab-mode auth.

# Documents / categories

- Transcripts (restricted by default)
- Quote bank
- Metaprompts
- Distillations
- Model response batches

# Page layout

- Search bar at top
- Filter chips (All, Transcripts, Distillations, Metaprompts, Responses)
- Document cards in responsive grid

Document card fields:
- document title
- type badge
- brief description/excerpt
- word count / estimated read time
- access state (public vs locked)

# Implementation notes

- Prefer a curated doc registry (avoid arbitrary filesystem browsing).
- Ensure doc routing cannot be used to read arbitrary files outside the allowlist.

# Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Public mode lists only allowlisted docs (or locked stubs) and never serves restricted content.
- [ ] Search filters results.
- [ ] Type filters work.
- [ ] Links navigate to document viewer for allowed docs.
- [ ] Locked docs show a clear "how to unlock" UX.

 {"id":"brenner_bot-7lf","title":"Content: Define Brenner Operators glossary entries (14 terms)","description":"# Brenner Operators Glossary Entries

## Goal
Write glossary entries for all 14 Brenner operators. Each needs:
- term: Display name with symbol
- short: One-liner for tooltip
- long: Plain-English explanation
- analogy: "Think of it like..." for non-scientists
- why: Relevance to scientific discovery
- related: 2-4 related operator/concept keys

## Terms to Define

### 1. level-split (⊘)
Separate program from interpreter; message from machine.
Example: DNA (program) vs ribosome (interpreter); "chastity vs impotence" distinction.

### 2. recode (𝓛)
Change representation/coordinates to make problem tractable.
Example: 3D protein folding → 1D genetic sequence problem.

### 3. invariant-extract (≡)
Find what survives coarse operations; use physics to kill cartoons.
Example: What survives across species = fundamental; what varies = implementation.

### 4. exclusion-test (✂)
Design experiments that eliminate whole model families at once.
Example: "If overlapping code, adjacent amino acid pairs would be forbidden."

### 5. object-transpose (⟂)
Change organism/system until the decisive experiment becomes cheap.
Example: Switch from mammals to C. elegans for developmental biology.

### 6. amplify (↑)
Use selection, dominance, regime switches to get "across the room" differences.
Example: Choose systems where signal is 50-70% of total, not 0.1%.

### 7. cross-domain (⊕)
Import tools/encodings from other fields to break monopolies.
Example: Apply information theory to molecular biology.

### 8. paradox-hunt (◊)
Use contradictions as beacons; start where the model can't be true.
Example: "How can DNA be 1mm in a 1μm cell?" → folding mechanism.

### 9. exception-quarantine (ΔE)
Isolate anomalies explicitly without hiding or destroying coherent theory.
Example: Note exceptions in ledger; don't sweep under carpet.

### 10. dephase (∿)
Work out of phase with scientific fashion; stay in opening game.
Example: Avoid crowded fields; find problems others haven't noticed.

### 11. theory-kill (†)
Drop hypotheses aggressively when nature says no.
Example: "Don't fall in love with your theory; be willing to kill it."

### 12. materialize (⌂)
Compile stories into decision procedures: "What would I see if this were true?"
Example: Transform verbal theory into concrete experimental prediction.

### 13. diy (🔧)
Build what you need; don't wait for infrastructure.
Example: Brenner's crude apparatus that worked vs elegant apparatus never built.

### 14. scale-check (⊞)
Calculate; stay imprisoned in physics; filter impossible cartoons.
Example: "Can this actually work at the relevant scale and timescale?"

## Writing Guidelines
- Avoid jargon in definitions (recursive problem!)
- Use concrete examples from Brenner's work
- Make analogies accessible to educated non-scientists
- Reference transcript §n anchors where applicable

## Deliverable
Add entries to `apps/web/src/lib/jargon.ts` under "operators" category.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-30T00:58:12.236001616-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-30T01:18:26.996180699-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-30T01:18:26.996180699-05:00","close_reason":"Enhanced all 19 Brenner operators with transcript §n anchors and added 3 derived operators (Quickie, HAL, Potency-Check)","labels":["content","glossary"]}
